Claude Code writes a file or runs a command, it asks for your approval. On a small task that is fine. On anything complex, you spend half the session clicking approve on things you have stopped reading.</p><p>Before Auto Mode existed, the only way around this was running:</p><p>claude --dangerously-skip-permissions</p><p>The name tells you everything. It removes all permission checks entirely. Fast, but reckless on a real machine with real files, API keys, and credentials sitting around.</p><div class=\\\"captioned-image-container\\\"><figure><a class=\\\"image-link image2 Claude makes permission decisions on your behalf, with a safety classifier reviewing each action before it runs. Safe actions proceed automatically. Risky ones get blocked, and Claude tries a different approach without interrupting you.</p><p>How to enable:</p><p>\u2192 Run claude --enable-auto-mode in your terminal<br>\u2192 Press Shift+Tab to cycle to Auto Mode in your session<br>\u2192 The current mode shows in the status bar</p><p>Tip: Auto Mode does not mean fully unattended. If the classifier repeatedly blocks an action, it will still pause and ask you. Think of it as Claude handling the routine approvals so you only get interrupted when something genuinely needs your attention.</p><div><hr></div><h3><strong>The Optimal Workflow</strong></h3><p>Most people jump straight into prompting. That is the wrong approach.</p><h4>The workflow:</h4><ol><li><p>/plan: Describe what you want. Review the plan before approving anything.</p></li><li><p>Be specific about the outcome. Name the format, the structure, the file type.</p></li><li><p>Let Claude break it into steps. Read them. This is where you catch misunderstandings before they cost you time.</p></li><li><p>Make one or two changes, then approve.</p></li><li><p>Let Claude run the whole task without interrupting it.</p></li><li><p>Refine the output in the same session.</p></li></ol><p>This is what this looks like in practice. Start simple, then layer in as much as you want.</p><h4>Step 1: The basic version.</h4><blockquote><p>/plan Scrape my last 20 LinkedIn posts using Apify, identify the 5 with the highest engagement, and rewrite each one as a 60-second Instagram Reel script. Format each script with a hook, three key points, and a CTA. Save each as a separate file in my Reels folder.</p></blockquote><div class=\\\"captioned-image-container\\\"><figure><a class=\\\"image-link image2 is-viewable-img\\\" target=\\\"_blank\\\" Not Notion, not a generic tool. Yours.</p><p>Each step builds on the last. You run the same basic workflow and add layers depending on how much signal you want feeding into the output.</p><p>The reason all of this works is the CLAUDE.md. Claude already knows your voice, your audience, and your content style before it writes a word. The plan keeps the task scoped. The approval step means no surprises.</p><p>And if the output is not quite right, you do not edit the file manually. You just tell Claude what you did not like in plain language. \u201CThe hooks are too long.\u201D \u201CStop using bullet points in the scripts.\u201D \u201CAlways include a posting time suggestion.\u201D Claude updates the CLAUDE.md there and then. Every future run reflects the change.</p><p>Tip: type /effort high before a complex task. Claude reasons more deeply before acting. For quick fixes, leave it on default.</p><div><hr></div><h3><strong>Skills and Automations</strong></h3><p>The workflow above took one session to build. Skills turn it into one command you run forever.</p><p>A SKILL.md file defines the entire workflow. Every step, every instruction, every output format. You run it once to build it, then trigger it anytime with a single command.</p><p>So instead of typing that full /plan prompt every week, you type:</p><blockquote><p>/linkedin-to-reels</p></blockquote><p>Claude runs the whole thing. Scrapes your posts, ranks by engagement, writes the scripts, saves the files. Same output every time, without you typing a word.</p><h4>How to create the Skill from the session you just ran:</h4><p>At the end of any session that produced a workflow you want to repeat, type:</p><blockquote><p>Write a Skill for this workflow.</p></blockquote><p>Claude writes the SKILL.md based on exactly what you both just built together. Save it to ./claude/skills/ and it is ready to run in any future session.</p><div class=\\\"captioned-image-container\\\"><figure><a class=\\\"image-link image2 is-viewable-img\\\" get them working before adding more. Every connected server adds context Claude has to manage in each session. More is not better. Connect the tools you open every day and ignore the rest.</p><div><hr></div><h3><strong>Parallel Agents</strong></h3><p>Split tasks across multiple agents running at the same time. Add \u201Cuse subagents\u201D to your prompt and Claude spawns parallel workers.</p><p>How it works:</p><p>\u2192 One orchestrator agent manages the task<br>\u2192 Sub-agents work on separate components simultaneously<br>\u2192 Results merge back into the main session</p><p>Building on the Reels workflow, this is where it gets fast. Instead of Claude scraping LinkedIn, then scraping Instagram, then researching trending Reels one after another, you add \u201Cuse subagents\u201D and all three run at the same time.</p><blockquote><p>/plan Use subagents to run these three research streams in parallel: scrape my last 20 LinkedIn posts via Apify, scrape my last 10 Instagram Reels via Apify, and pull the top 20 trending Reels in my niche this week. When all three are done, cross-reference the findings and write the top 5 scripts.</p></blockquote><div class=\\\"captioned-image-container\\\"><figure><a class=\\\"image-link image2 simultaneously. Claude synthesises the results when all three finish. The whole job takes a fraction of the time.</p><p>Tip: combine parallel agents with /plan. Plan the task first, then let Claude decompose it into parallel sub-tasks automatically. This is where Code starts feeling like a team, not a tool.</p><div><hr></div><h3><strong>Remote Control and Channels</strong></h3><p>Control your Claude Code session from your phone or another device. Similar to Dispatch in Cowork, but for coding sessions.</p><p>\u2192 Remote Control: Monitor and interact with sessions from Desktop, iOS, or Android<br>\u2192 Channels: Control Claude Code via Discord and Telegram as an alternative to the terminal</p><p>With the /linkedin-to-reels workflow running on your desktop, Remote Control means you can kick it off from your phone on the way to a meeting and check in on progress without sitting at your Mac. If something needs steering mid-run, you handle it from wherever you are.</p><div class=\\\"captioned-image-container\\\"><figure><a class=\\\"image-link image2 is-viewable-img\\\" Discord. You message the bot, it scrapes your LinkedIn posts, asks a couple of quick questions about format and length, and delivers a finished Reel script straight into the chat. Two messages. Done.</p><p>That is one example. The broader point is that once Claude Code is set up, almost anything repetitive, automatable, or delegatable can be handled this way. You do not need to be at your desk. You do not need to open a terminal. You send a message and come back to finished work.</p><p>If you have a task that needs automating, there is a good chance Claude Code can do it.</p><p>Tip: If you manage a team, run Claude Code from a shared Discord or Telegram channel. Everyone sees what Claude is building without needing terminal access.</p><div><hr></div><p>For reference, every major Claude release this year:</p><p>\u2192 Jan 7: Claude Code 2.1.0 shipped<br>\u2192 Jan 12: Cowork launched (Mac, Max plan)<br>\u2192 Jan 16: Cowork opened to Pro users<br>\u2192 Feb 5: Opus 4.6 launched (1M context)<br>\u2192 Feb 17: Sonnet 4.6 launched<br>\u2192 Feb 19: Claude in PowerPoint launched<br>\u2192 Feb 20: Claude Code Security launched<br>\u2192 Feb 24: Remote Control launched<br>\u2192 Feb 24: Plugin marketplace launched<br>\u2192 Feb 25: Scheduled Tasks in Cowork<br>\u2192 Mar 2: Memory became free for all users<br>\u2192 Mar 6: Enterprise Marketplace launched<br>\u2192 Mar 11: Excel and PowerPoint sync<br>\u2192 Mar 12: Interactive charts in chat<br>\u2192 Mar 17: Dispatch (phone to desktop)<br>\u2192 Mar 23: Computer Use went live (macOS)<br>\u2192 Mar 24: Claude Code Auto Mode launched</p><p>17 releases in under 3 months. And they are not slowing down.</p><p>Most people read a list like that and feel behind. I want you to feel the opposite. Every feature in this newsletter is available to you today. The gap between where you are and where you could be is not about access. It is about setup.</p><p>Pick one thing from this newsletter and set it up this week.</p><p>The people pulling the furthest ahead are not using some secret version of Claude. They are using more of the product than everyone else.</p><p>This is the most comprehensive breakdown of Claude I have published. If it saved you time or changed how you think about the product, share it with someone who is still typing into a chat box and calling it an AI strategy.</p><p><em>Stay curious, stay human, and start treating Claude like the operating system it has become.</em></p><p>\u2014 Charlie</p><div><hr></div><h3>Events</h3><p>If you want to meet in person, I have two coming up.</p><p><a href=\\\"https://www.influencerstrategists.com/creator-events/fuji\\\">AI in the Creator Economy</a> is on April 9 at the FUJIFILM House of Photography.
